en.extract:
The mangrove swallow (Tachycineta albilinea) is a passerine bird in the swallow family that breeds in coastal regions from Mexico through Central America to Panama. It has blue-green upperparts, blackish flight feathers, a white rump, a black tail, and white underparts. It can be identified by the supraloral white streak, the white line near its eye, which only occurs in two other species of Tachycineta: the violet-green swallow and the white-rumped swallow. The sexes, although similar in plumage, differ slightly in size. The juveniles have grey-brown upperparts and white-washed underparts. This swallow's song is generally described as a soft trilling, with a rolled jeerrt call, and a sharp alarm note.
The mangrove swallow is very territorial when breeding, much like the related tree swallow. Its nest is normally built in a hole or crevice near water and less than 2 metres (7 ft) above the ground. This species usually feeds alone when breeding, but will feed in groups when not. It normally forages closer to the nest when hunting for its chicks, but will go much further when foraging for itself. In between foraging attempts, it is frequently seen perching near water. It is an aerial insectivore and eats unusually large prey for its size.
With an estimated population of at least 500,000 individuals, the mangrove swallow is classified as a species of least concern by the International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN). Its numbers are decreasing, although not fast enough for it to be classified as vulnerable. Little is known about the predation of this species, although it is a host of Sternostoma hirundinis, a type of nasal mite. It has also been known to lose nests both to termites and black flies.


Taxonomy and etymology
The mangrove swallow was formally described in 1863 as Petrochelidon albilinea by American amateur ornithologist George Newbold Lawrence. Its current genus, Tachycineta, was originally described in 1850 by the German ornithologist Jean Cabanis. The genus name Tachycineta is from Ancient Greek takhukinetos, "moving quickly", and the specific albilinea is from Latin albus, "white", and linea, "line".
The Tachycineta species are members of the swallow family of birds, and are placed in the Hirundininae subfamily, which comprises all swallows and martins except the very distinctive river martins. DNA sequence studies suggest that there are three major groupings within the Hirundininae, broadly correlating with the type of nest built. These groups are the "core martins", including burrowing species like the sand martin, the "nest-adopters", which are birds that utilise natural cavities, and the "mud nest builders" such as the Delichon house martins. The Tachycineta species belong to the "nest-adopter" group.
All nine Tachycineta species have glossy blue or green backs and white underparts, but the five species with white rumps – the mangrove swallow, Tumbes swallow, white-winged swallow, white-rumped swallow and Chilean swallow – are particularly closely related, the first three and the last two forming two superspecies. The Tumbes swallow of coastal Peru was formerly considered to be a subspecies of the mangrove swallow, but its calls, behaviour, and cytochrome b data indicate that it should be considered a separate species. It is also differentiated from the mangrove swallow by its lack of a supraloral white line and by its slight difference in size.


Description

A relatively small swallow, the mangrove swallow is 11–12 centimetres (4.3–4.7 in) long and weighs about 14 grams (0.5 oz). The adult has iridescent blue-green upperparts, white underparts, rump, and undertail- and wing-coverts, and blackish tail and flight feathers. The feathers are greener when fresh and bluer when worn. The white underparts sometimes have faint, dark shaft streaks. The bill is small and black, about 11 millimetres (0.43 in) long. The iris is a dark brown, and the tarsus and toes range in colour from black to fuscous-brown. The lores are black and have a thin white line above them. Two other species of Tachycineta have this distinctive feature: the violet-green swallow and the white-rumped swallow. The adult's tail is only slightly forked. The sexes are similar, although they differ slightly in size. Compared to the male, the female has a slightly longer tail and slightly shorter wings. The juvenile is dull grey-brown above and grey-brown washed white below. Occasionally, the juvenile's upperparts have a subtle, greenish gloss.
The call of the mangrove swallow is a rolled "jeerrrt", or a "chriet". The song is frequently described as a soft trilling. It uses a short sharp alarm note.


Distribution
This swallow is native to Mexico and all of Central America (Belize, Guatemala, Honduras, El Salvador, Nicaragua, Costa Rica and Panama). It is usually found near low-lying bodies of water and mangrove forests, which gave rise to the common name. It has also been recorded in intertidal zones. In Mexico, it is not typically found above 600 metres (2,000 ft). In Costa Rica, it has been found to occur as high as 1,000 metres (3,300 ft), but it normally occurs between 500 metres (1,600 ft) and sea level. It is rarely found in the highlands. The mangrove swallow is also vagrant to the United States, where it was first recorded in 2002, in Florida. Although the mangrove swallow is a full-time resident of its range, there are probably some post-breeding movements.


Behaviour
The mangrove swallow is closely associated with fairly still, open water, and is often found in small flocks over rivers or lakes when not breeding. Its flight path is normally direct and low over water. It flies with quick wingbeats and some gliding. In between foraging attempts, it can frequently be seen to perch.


Habitat

The mangrove swallow is a solitary bird; its nests are not found closer than 50 metres (160 ft) away from each other, and usually have about 300 metres (1,000 ft) separating them. The nest itself is built in natural or artificial cavities near water, usually in a tree stump or dead tree. It is also not uncommon for it to nest in nest boxes. The nest is a few centimetres deep and made with grass, fine stems, moss, and a few leaves and sticks. It is padded with feathers. The nest is normally found to occur below 2 metres (7 ft). While the nest is built relatively low, it is still normally above the water level of a typical flood. Sites include tree holes and crevices in rock or bridges.


Breeding
During its five-month breeding season, the mangrove swallow typically lays two broods. The data suggests that the breeding season is from approximately January to July in Central America, and from around March to June or July in Mexico. Like the related tree swallow, this species is very aggressive to other hirundines when breeding and is very territorial.
This swallow is socially monogamous, with some extra-pair mating. For example, about 15% of nestlings and about 25% of broods are developed through extra-pair paternity. A nest with extra-pair young is, on average, about 430 metres (1,410 ft) away from its nearest neighbour. Extra-pair young are also correlated with the breeding synchrony index, or the percentage of females fertile simultaneously. The fact that the laying of eggs is not synchronized within a population is due to the long breeding season of the mangrove swallow. This swallow also displays paternity assurance behaviour. For example, males follow fertile females more often than females follow males. The period of fertility is from six days before the first egg is laid until the second to last egg is laid.
The clutch is three to five white eggs which hatch in 17 days. On average the eggs measure 17.3 mm × 12.8 mm (0.68 in × 0.50 in) and weigh about 1.6 grams (0.056 oz). The eggs hatch asynchronously, with about a 60% hatchability. The nestlings are fed by both parents for 23–27 days to fledging. Usually, there is only one successful fledgling per nest.


Diet
The mangrove swallow subsists primarily on a diet of small, flying insects, including large species such as dragonflies and bees. The prey it feeds on is large for a bird of its size. This swallow usually feeds close over bays, lakes, and large rivers, but sometimes can be found to forage 30 metres (98 ft) or more above the water. It normally forages in the early morning and late afternoon, with nestlings being fed just after sunrise and before sunset. Non-breeding mangrove swallows normally forage in small flocks, although when breeding, it feeds alone or in pairs.
The mangrove swallow typically stays within about 100 metres (330 ft) of its nest when it is foraging for the nestlings. But, when hunting for itself, it has been recorded to go as far out as 200 metres (660 ft) from its nest. Ordinarily, it will feed within its own territory.


Predators and parasites
Not much is known about the predators and parasites of the mangrove swallow, although it has been known to lose nests to termites and black flies. The black flies are mainly local and typically only affect artificial nest boxes. The prevalence of the flies is linked to nearby streams and is dependent on weather conditions. This swallow is a host to the parasite Sternostoma hirundinis, a type of nasal mite.


Status
As of 2016, the mangrove swallow is classified as least concern by the IUCN. There is some evidence that the population is decreasing. This is probably due to habitat loss or the use of pesticides. The justification for classifying this species as least concern is its large population, estimated to be greater than 500,000 individuals, and its extremely large range, estimated to be about 3,170,000 square kilometres (1,220,000 mi2). Although the population is decreasing, it is not decreasing rapidly enough to be classified as a vulnerable species.


References

[[File:Mangrove swallow (Tachycineta albilinea) immature.jpg|thumb|Immature, [[Panama]]]]
The '''mangrove swallow''' ('''''Tachycineta albilinea''''') is a [[passerine]] [[bird]] in the [[swallow]] family that breeds in coastal regions from [[Mexico]] through [[Central America]] to [[Panama]]. It has blue-green upperparts, blackish [[flight feather]]s, a white rump, a black tail, and white underparts. It can be identified by the {{birdgloss|supraloral}} white streak, the white line near its eye, which only occurs in two other species of ''[[Tachycineta]]'': the [[violet-green swallow]] and the [[white-rumped swallow]]. The sexes, although similar in [[plumage]], differ slightly in size. The juveniles have grey-brown upperparts and white-washed underparts. This swallow's [[bird vocalization|song]] is generally described as a soft trilling, with a rolled ''jeerrt'' call, and a sharp alarm note.

The mangrove swallow is very [[territory (animal)|territorial]] when breeding, much like the related [[tree swallow]]. Its [[bird nest|nest]] is normally built in a hole or crevice near water and less than {{convert|2|m|ft|sigfig=1}} above the ground. This species usually feeds alone when breeding, but will feed in groups when not. It normally forages closer to the nest when [[Bird#Parental care and fledging|hunting for its chicks]], but will go much further when foraging for itself. In between foraging attempts, it is frequently seen perching near water. It is an aerial [[insectivore]] and eats unusually large [[predation|prey]] for its size.

With an estimated population of at least 500,000 individuals, the mangrove swallow is classified as a species of [[least-concern species|least concern]] by the [[International Union for Conservation of Nature]] (IUCN). Its numbers are decreasing, although not fast enough for it to be classified as [[vulnerable species|vulnerable]]. Little is known about the predation of this species, although it is a [[host (biology)|host]] of ''[[Sternostoma hirundinis]]'', a type of nasal [[mite]]. It has also been known to lose nests both to [[termite]]s and [[black fly|black flies]].

== Taxonomy and etymology ==
The mangrove swallow was formally [[species description|described]] in 1863 as ''Petrochelidon albilinea'' by American amateur [[ornithologist]] [[George Newbold Lawrence]].<ref name= lawrence>{{cite journal | last = Lawrence | first = George Newbold Lawrence | title = Catalogue of a Collection of Birds, made in New Granada, by James McLeannan, Esq., of New York, with Notes and Descriptions of New Species. Part IV | journal = Annals of the Lyceum of Natural History of New York | volume = 8 | page = 2 | year = 1863 | url = https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/item/54331#page/22/mode/1up | access-date = 2018-01-14 | archive-date = 2017-08-10 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20170810010408/http://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/item/54331#page/22/mode/1up | url-status = live }}</ref> Its current [[genus]], ''[[Tachycineta]]'', was originally described in 1850 by the German ornithologist [[Jean Cabanis]].<ref name=cabanis>{{cite book | last1 = Cabanis | first1 = Jean | author-link = Jean Cabanis | title = Museum Heineanum: Verzeichniss der ornithologischen Sammlung des Oberamtmann Ferdinand Heine auf Gut St. Burchard vor Halberstatdt | publisher = Independently commissioned by R. Frantz | volume = 1 | year = 1850 | location = Halberstadt | page = 48 | language = de | url = https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/item/196124#page/64/mode/1up | access-date = 2018-01-14 | archive-date = 2017-10-25 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20171025185004/https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/item/196124#page/64/mode/1up | url-status = live }}</ref> The genus name ''Tachycineta'' is from [[Ancient Greek]] ''takhukinetos'', "moving quickly", and the specific ''albilinea'' is from [[Latin]] ''albus'', "white", and ''linea'', "line".<ref name=job>{{cite book | last= Jobling | first= James A. | year= 2010| title= The Helm Dictionary of Scientific Bird Names | url= https://archive.org/details/Helm_Dictionary_of_Scientific_Bird_Names_by_James_A._Jobling | publisher= Christopher Helm | location = London | isbn = 978-1-4081-2501-4 | pages = [https://archive.org/details/Helm_Dictionary_of_Scientific_Bird_Names_by_James_A._Jobling/page/n39 39], 377}}</ref>

The ''Tachycineta'' species are members of the [[swallow]] family of birds, and are placed in the Hirundininae subfamily, which comprises all swallows and martins except the very distinctive [[river martin]]s. [[DNA sequence]] studies suggest that there are three major groupings within the Hirundininae, broadly correlating with the type of nest built.<ref name= sheldon>{{cite journal | last=Sheldon | first=Frederick H. |author-link =Fred Sheldon (ornithologist)|author2=Whittingham, Linda A. |author3=Moyle, Robert G. |author4=Slikas, Beth |author5= Winkler, David W. | year= 2005 | title=Phylogeny of swallows (Aves: Hirundinidae) estimated from nuclear and mitochondrial DNA | journal = [[Molecular Phylogenetics and Evolution]] | volume=35 | issue =1 | pages= 254–270 | doi=10.1016/j.ympev.2004.11.008 | pmid=15737595}}</ref> These groups are the "core martins", including burrowing species like the [[sand martin]], the "nest-adopters", which are birds that utilise natural cavities, and the "mud nest builders" such as the ''[[Delichon]]'' house martins. The ''Tachycineta'' species belong to the "nest-adopter" group.<ref name= sheldon2>{{cite journal | doi=10.1073/pnas.90.12.5705 | last=Winkler | first=David W. |author2=Sheldon, Frederick H. | year=1993 | title= Evolution of nest construction in swallows (Hirundinidae): A molecular phylogenetic perspective | journal= Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences USA| volume=90 | pages=5705–5707 | pmid=8516319 | issue=12 | pmc=46790| bibcode=1993PNAS...90.5705W | doi-access=free }}</ref>

All nine ''Tachycineta'' species have glossy blue or green backs and white underparts, but the five species with white rumps – the mangrove swallow, [[Tumbes swallow]], [[white-winged swallow]], [[white-rumped swallow]] and [[Chilean swallow]] – are particularly closely related, the first three and the last two forming two [[superspecies]]. The Tumbes swallow of coastal Peru was formerly considered to be a [[subspecies]] of the mangrove swallow, but its calls, behaviour, and [[cytochrome b]] data indicate that it should be considered a separate species.<ref name="hbwfamily">{{cite journal |last1=Turner |first1=Angela |editor1-last=del Hoyo |editor1-first=Josep |editor2-last=Elliott |editor2-first=Andrew |editor3-last=Sargatal |editor3-first=Jordi |editor4-last=Christie |editor4-first=David A. |editor5-last=de Juana |editor5-first=Eduardo |title=Family Hirundinidae: Swallows and Martins |journal=Handbook of the Birds of the World Alive |url=http://www.hbw.com/family/swallows-and-martins-hirundinidae |publisher=Lynx Edicions |location=Barcelona |doi=10.2173/bow.hirund2.01 |s2cid=241223129 |access-date=13 October 2016 |url-access=subscription |archive-date=5 October 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231005051122/https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/hirund2/cur/introduction |url-status=live }}</ref> It is also differentiated from the mangrove swallow by its lack of a supraloral white line and by its slight difference in size.<ref name="Turner" />

== Description ==
[[File:Mangrove swallow (Tachycineta albilinea) Peten 2.jpg|thumb|Front view ]]
A relatively small swallow, the mangrove swallow is {{convert|11|-|12|cm|in}} long and weighs about {{convert|14|g|oz|sigfig=1}}. The adult has [[iridescence|iridescent]] blue-green upperparts, white underparts, rump, and [[Covert feather#Tail coverts|undertail-]] and [[Covert feather#Wing coverts|wing-coverts]], and blackish tail and flight feathers. The feathers are greener when fresh and bluer when worn. The white underparts sometimes have faint, dark shaft streaks.<ref name="hbw" /> The [[beak|bill]] is small and black, about {{convert|11|mm|in}} long. The iris is a dark brown, and the [[bird anatomy|tarsus]] and toes range in colour from black to fuscous-brown. The [[lore (anatomy)#Ornithology|lores]] are black and have a thin white line above them. Two other species of ''Tachycineta'' have this distinctive feature: the [[violet-green swallow]] and the white-rumped swallow. The adult's tail is only slightly forked. The sexes are similar, although they [[sexual dimorphism|differ slightly]] in size. Compared to the male, the female has a slightly longer tail and slightly shorter wings. The juvenile is dull grey-brown above and grey-brown washed white below.<ref name="hbw">{{cite journal |last1=Turner |first1=Angela |editor1-last=del Hoyo |editor1-first=Josep |editor2-last=Elliott |editor2-first=Andrew |editor3-last=Sargatal |editor3-first=Jordi |editor4-last=Christie |editor4-first=David A. |editor5-last=de Juana |editor5-first=Eduardo |year=2020 |title=Mangrove Swallow (''Tachycineta albilinea'') |journal=Handbook of the Birds of the World Alive |url=http://www.hbw.com/species/mangrove-swallow-tachycineta-albilinea |publisher=Lynx Edicions |location=Barcelona |doi=10.2173/bow.manswa1.01 |s2cid=216248424 |access-date=November 7, 2016 |url-access=subscription |archive-date=October 5, 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231005055736/https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/manswa1/cur/introduction |url-status=live }}</ref><ref name="cornell">{{cite web|title=Mangrove Swallow (''Tachycineta albilinea'')|work=Neotropical Birds Online|url=https://neotropical.birds.cornell.edu/portal/species/overview?p_p_spp=523436|last=Buderman|first=F. E.|year=2010|access-date=November 6, 2016|publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology|editor-last=Schulenberg|editor-first=T. S.|archive-date=December 20, 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161220203458/http://neotropical.birds.cornell.edu/portal/species/overview?p_p_spp=523436|url-status=live}}</ref> Occasionally, the juvenile's upperparts have a subtle, greenish gloss.<ref name="Turner">{{cite book |title=Swallows & Martins: An Identification Guide and Handbook |last1=Turner |first1=Angela K |last2=Rose |first2=Chris |location=Boston |publisher=Houghton Mifflin |year=1989 |isbn=0-395-51174-7 |url-access=registration |url=https://archive.org/details/swallowsmartinsi00turn }} p101–102</ref>

The call of the mangrove swallow is a rolled ''"jeerrrt"'', or a ''"chriet"''. The song is frequently described as a soft trilling.<ref name="hbw" /><ref name="cornell" /> It uses a short sharp alarm note.<ref name="Turner" />

== Distribution ==
This swallow is native to [[Mexico]] and all of [[Central America]] ([[Belize]], [[Guatemala]], [[Honduras]], [[El Salvador]], [[Nicaragua]], [[Costa Rica]] and [[Panama]]). It is usually found near low-lying bodies of water and [[mangrove]] forests, which gave rise to the [[common name]]. It has also been recorded in [[intertidal zone]]s. In Mexico, it is not typically found above {{convert|600|m|ft}}.<ref name="iucn status 19 November 2021" /> In Costa Rica, it has been found to occur as high as {{convert|1000|m|ft}}, but it normally occurs between {{convert|500|m|ft}} and sea level. It is rarely found in the highlands. The mangrove swallow is also [[vagrancy (biology)|vagrant]] to the [[List of birds of the United States|United States]], where it was first recorded in 2002, in [[Florida]].<ref name="cornell" /> Although the mangrove swallow is a full-time resident of its [[range (biology)|range]], there are probably some post-breeding movements.<ref name="Turner" /><ref name="cornell" />

== Behaviour ==

The mangrove swallow is closely associated with fairly still, open water, and is often found in small [[flock (birds)|flocks]] over rivers or lakes when not breeding.<ref name="cornell" /> Its flight path is normally direct and low over water. It flies with quick wingbeats and some [[gliding flight|gliding]].<ref name="hbw" /> In between foraging attempts, it can frequently be seen to perch.<ref name="Ricklefs">{{cite journal|last=Ricklefs|first=Robert|year=1971|title=Foraging behavior of Mangrove Swallows at Barro Colorado Island|author-link=Robert E. Ricklefs|journal=The Auk|volume=88|issue=3|pages=635–651|url=https://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/auk/v088n03/p0635-p0651.pdf|access-date=2016-11-06|archive-date=2020-07-14|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200714050552/https://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/auk/v088n03/p0635-p0651.pdf|url-status=live}}</ref>

=== Habitat ===
[[File:Mangrove Swallow.jpg|thumb|Mangrove swallow perching next to the [[Sarapiqui River]]]]
The mangrove swallow is a solitary bird; its nests are not found closer than {{convert|50|m|ft}} away from each other, and usually have about {{convert|300|m|ft|sigfig=1}} separating them. The nest itself is built in natural or artificial cavities near water, usually in a tree stump or dead tree.<ref name="hbw" /> It is also not uncommon for it to nest in [[nest box]]es.<ref name="Dyrcz2000">{{cite journal|last=Dyrcz|first=Andrzej|title=Nesting of the Mangrove Swallow (''Tachycineta albilinea'') In Nests of the Rusty-Margined (''Myozetetes cayanensis'') and Social (''M. similis'') Flycatchers|journal=Ornitologia Neotropical|volume=11|issue=1|year=2000|pages=83–84|url=https://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/on/v011n01/p0083-p0084.pdf|access-date=December 17, 2016|archive-date=July 14, 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200714050230/https://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/on/v011n01/p0083-p0084.pdf|url-status=live}}</ref> The nest is a few centimetres deep and made with grass, fine stems, moss, and a few leaves and sticks. It is padded with feathers. The nest is normally found to occur below {{convert|2|m|ft|sigfig=1}}. While the nest is built relatively low, it is still normally above the water level of a typical flood.<ref name="Turner" /> Sites include tree holes and crevices in rock or bridges.<ref name="hbw" />

=== Breeding ===
During its five-month breeding season, the mangrove swallow typically lays two broods.<ref name="Ricklefs" /> The data suggests that the breeding season is from approximately January to July in Central America, and from around March to June or July in Mexico.<ref name="Turner" /> Like the related tree swallow, this species is very aggressive to other hirundines when breeding and is very territorial.<ref name="hbw" />

This swallow is [[Monogamy#Varieties of monogamy in biology|socially monogamous]], with some [[Extra-pair copulation|extra-pair mating]]. For example, about 15% of nestlings and about 25% of broods are developed through extra-pair paternity.<ref name="hbw" /> A nest with extra-pair young is, on average, about {{convert|430|m|ft}} away from its nearest neighbour. Extra-pair young are also correlated with the [[reproductive synchrony|breeding synchrony]] index, or the percentage of females fertile simultaneously. The fact that the laying of eggs is not synchronized within a population is due to the long breeding season of the mangrove swallow.<ref name="hbw" /> This swallow also displays [[Sexual conflict#Sperm competition|paternity assurance behaviour]]. For example, males follow fertile females more often than females follow males. The period of fertility is from six days before the first egg is laid until the second to last egg is laid.<ref name="Quinn1999">{{cite journal|last1=Moore|first1=Owen R.|last2=Stutchbury|first2=Bridget J. M.|last3=Quinn|first3=James S.|title=Extrapair mating system of an asynchronously breeding tropical songbird: the Mangrove Swallow|journal=The Auk|url=https://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/auk/v116n04/p1039-p1046.pdf|volume=116|issue=4|year=1999|pages=1039–1046|issn=0004-8038|doi=10.2307/4089683|jstor=4089683|access-date=2017-01-14|archive-date=2020-07-14|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200714050343/https://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/auk/v116n04/p1039-p1046.pdf|url-status=live}}</ref>

The [[clutch (eggs)|clutch]] is three to five white eggs which hatch in 17 days.<ref name="hbw" /> On average the eggs measure {{convert|17.3|x|12.8|mm|in|abbr=on}} and weigh about {{convert|1.6|g|oz}}. The eggs hatch asynchronously, with about a 60% hatchability. The nestlings are fed by both parents for 23–27 days to [[fledge|fledging]].<ref name="Turner" /><ref name="hbw" /> Usually, there is only one successful fledgling per nest.<ref name="Dyrcz1984">{{cite journal|last1=Dyrcz|first1=Andrzej|title=Breeding biology of the Mangrove Swallow ''Tachycineta albilinea'' and the Grey-breasted Martin ''Progne chalybea'' at Barro Colorado Island, Panama|journal=Ibis|volume=126|issue=1|year=1984|pages=59–66|issn=0019-1019|doi=10.1111/j.1474-919X.1984.tb03664.x}}</ref>

=== Diet ===
The mangrove swallow subsists primarily on a diet of small, flying [[insectivore|insects]], including large species such as [[dragonfly|dragonflies]] and [[bee]]s. The prey it feeds on is large for a bird of its size. This swallow usually feeds close over bays, lakes, and large rivers, but sometimes can be found to forage {{convert|30|m|ft}} or more above the water.<ref name="Ricklefs" /> It normally forages in the early morning and late afternoon, with nestlings being fed just after sunrise and before sunset. Non-breeding mangrove swallows normally forage in small flocks, although when breeding, it feeds alone or in pairs.<ref name="hbw" />

The mangrove swallow typically stays within about {{convert|100|m|ft}} of its nest when it is foraging for the nestlings. But, when hunting for itself, it has been recorded to go as far out as {{convert|200|m|ft}} from its nest.<ref name="hbw" /><ref name="Ricklefs" /> Ordinarily, it will feed within its own territory.<ref name="hbwfamily" />

== Predators and parasites ==
Not much is known about the predators and parasites of the mangrove swallow, although it has been known to lose nests to termites and black flies.<ref name="hbw" /> The black flies are mainly local and typically only affect artificial nest boxes. The prevalence of the flies is linked to nearby streams and is dependent on weather conditions.<ref name="parasitic">{{cite book | last = Atkinson | first = Carter | title = Parasitic Diseases of Wild Birds | url = https://archive.org/details/parasiticdisease00atki | url-access = limited | publisher = Wiley-Blackwell | location = Ames, Iowa | year = 2008 | isbn = 978-0-8138-0462-0 |page=[https://archive.org/details/parasiticdisease00atki/page/n545 543]}}</ref> This swallow is a host to the parasite ''[[Sternostoma hirundinis]]'', a type of nasal mite.<ref name="spicer">{{cite journal|journal=Journal of Parasitic Diseases|volume=73|issue=2|year=1987|last=Spicer|first=Greg S.|publisher=American Society of Parasitologists|title=Prevalence and Host—parasite List of Some Nasal Mites From Birds (Acarina: Rhinonyssidae, Speleognathidae)|pages=259–264|access-date=December 18, 2016|url=http://userwww.sfsu.edu/gs/spicer/pages/spicerpdf/spicer1987para.pdf|archive-date=December 20, 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161220210040/http://userwww.sfsu.edu/gs/spicer/pages/spicerpdf/spicer1987para.pdf|url-status=live}}</ref>

== Status ==
As of 2016, the mangrove swallow is classified as [[least concern]] by the [[IUCN]]. There is some evidence that the population is decreasing. This is probably due to [[habitat destruction|habitat loss]] or the use of [[pesticide]]s.<ref name="hbw" /> The justification for classifying this species as least concern is its large population, estimated to be greater than 500,000 individuals, and its extremely large range, estimated to be about {{convert|3170000|km2|mi2|sigfig=3}}. Although the population is decreasing, it is not decreasing rapidly enough to be classified as a vulnerable species.<ref name="iucn status 19 November 2021" />
